defining_events.character_age_at_event: 2 — no direct memory, but parents' fear imprints
defining_events.developmental_impact: Background radiation of vulnerability in supposedly safe places

defining_events.event: Plano heroin crisis and teen deaths
defining_events.character_age_at_event: 4-5 — too young to understand, but absorbs adult anxiety
defining_events.developmental_impact: Community trauma that reshapes parenting culture toward control

defining_events.character_age_at_event: 5 — entering school system as lockdown drills begin
defining_events.developmental_impact: School as simultaneously safe haven and potential danger zone

defining_events.character_age_at_event: 7 — old enough to remember the TV, the teachers crying
defining_events.developmental_impact: End of background safety assumption; patriotism surge in Texas

defining_events.event: Telecom Corridor layoffs and dot-com bust
defining_events.character_age_at_event: 7-8 — may experience parent's job loss or neighbors' foreclosure
defining_events.developmental_impact: First crack in the suburban promise

defining_events.character_age_at_event: 11-12 — entering adolescence with digital identity layer
defining_events.developmental_impact: Identity formation becomes partly public and performative

economic_entry: Will enter adulthood during the Great Recession aftermath — credential inflation, gig economy, delayed milestones
relationship_to_prior_generation: Parents are Boomers or early Gen X — children of counterculture who became corporate suburbanites. The unspoken question: did they sell out, or grow up?
cohort_narrative: The last children to remember a world before the internet ate everything, old enough to feel the before and after.
